questions.to_find: The values of a and b printed after the call P(a, b, b) using call-by-reference

understand.plain: In call-by-reference, the formal parameters become aliases (references) to the actual arguments. When P is called as P(a, b, b), x refers to a, y refers to b, and z also refers to b. Any modification to x, y, or z inside P directly modifies the corresponding actual variable.

keywords.explain: The formal parameter is a reference (alias) to the actual argument. Changes to the formal parameter directly change the actual argument.
keywords.example: If P(a) is called by reference, then inside P, x := 5 sets a := 5 in the caller.

keywords.explain: Two or more names refer to the same memory location. Here y and z both reference b.
keywords.example: P(a, b, b) makes y and z both aliases of b; modifying z also overwrites any effect of modifying y.

understand.visual_alt: Procedure P called with P(a, b, b): x->a (value 2), y->b (value 3), z->b (same variable as y). After y:=y+1, b becomes 4. After z:=x+x, b becomes 2+2=4. Final: a=2, b=4.

given.aim: Trace the execution of P(a, b, b) under call-by-reference and find printed values of a and b.

terms.meaning: Initial values of the two integer variables before the call.
terms.example: a := 2 sets a to 2; b := 3 sets b to 3.
terms.connects: These are the actual arguments passed to P.

terms.meaning: x is bound to a, y is bound to b, z is also bound to b (same memory location).
terms.example: All three formal parameters point into the caller's frame.
terms.connects: Since y and z both alias b, the last assignment to z determines b's final value.

terms.meaning: Increments the variable aliased by y, i.e., b becomes b+1 = 4.
terms.connects: Intermediate update to b, but z := x+x will overwrite b again.

given.plan: Substitute the call-by-reference bindings: x->a, y->b, z->b. Execute the two statements inside P step by step, remembering that y and z are the same variable b. Then read the final values of a and b.

steps.title: Bind parameters by reference
steps.formula_raw: x -> a, y -> b, z -> b
steps.apply: The call P(a, b, b) binds: x is an alias for a (value 2), y is an alias for b (value 3), z is also an alias for b (same location as y).
steps.note: The critical observation is that y and z are aliases of the same variable b.

steps.title: Execute y := y + 1
steps.apply: y and b refer to the same location. b was 3, so b := 3 + 1 = 4. Now b = 4.

steps.title: Execute z := x + x
steps.apply: z refers to b, x refers to a. So b := a + a = 2 + 2 = 4. b is still 4 (coincidentally the same value).
steps.note: This overwrites the result of step 2. If a were different, z:=x+x could give a different result than y:=y+1.

steps.title: Read printed output
steps.note: a was never modified inside P since x was only read, not written.

questions.formula_note: Call-by-reference aliasing: when the same actual argument appears multiple times, all corresponding formal parameters share one memory location; the last write wins.

questions.answer: B. No, it is incorrect because the index i might change inside P, so A[i] at the end might be a different element than A[i] at the start.
questions.to_find: Whether the given implementation of call-by-value-result for P(A[i]) is correct

understand.plain: Call-by-value-result copies the value of the actual argument into a local variable at the start, executes the procedure, then copies the local variable's value back to the original location at the end. The question is: when the actual argument is A[i], what location should receive the copy-back? The implementation uses A[B[i]] with B[i] initially equal to i, but if i changes during P, the copy-back goes to the wrong array element.

keywords.explain: Also called copy-in/copy-out. The actual argument's value is copied into the formal parameter at call time. At return, the formal parameter's final value is copied back to the actual argument's location.
keywords.example: P(A[i]): copy A[i] into k at entry; on exit, copy k back into A[i] (the original location).

keywords.explain: In call-by-value-result, the address to write back to is typically determined at the time of the call (not at return). If i changes inside P, the address A[i] at return time is different from the address at call time.
keywords.example: If i=2 at call and i=5 at return, writing to A[i] at return writes to A[5] instead of A[2].

understand.visual_alt: Timeline: at call, i=2, A[2] is copied into z. Inside P, i is modified to 5. At return, the implementation copies z to A[B[i]] = A[B[5]] which may not be A[2].

given.aim: Determine if using an auxiliary array B (where B[i]=i initially) to record the copy-back address correctly implements call-by-value-result for P(A[i]).

terms.meaning: The array element whose value is passed into P and whose location should receive the result.
terms.connects: The copy-back address is the address of A[i] at call time, not at return time.

terms.meaning: Auxiliary array meant to record the index of the actual argument so the copy-back uses A[B[i]] = A[i].
terms.connects: The scheme assumes i does not change; if i changes inside P, B[i] no longer equals the original index.

terms.meaning: If P modifies i (as a global or through some side effect), the index used at copy-back differs from the index at call time.
terms.example: i starts at 2; P sets i := 5; copy-back writes to A[B[5]] instead of A[B[2]] = A[2].
terms.connects: This is the key flaw — the proposed implementation does not correctly preserve the original address.

given.plan: Standard call-by-value-result must evaluate the l-value (address) of the actual argument at call time and store it. Here the index i might change inside P, so using A[B[i]] at return time is wrong if i has changed. Check whether the auxiliary array B correctly captures the original address.

steps.title: Recall correct call-by-value-result semantics
steps.apply: At call: evaluate the address of A[i] (i.e., the index value at that moment, say i=j). Copy A[j] into local z. At return: write z back to A[j] (the address recorded at call time).
steps.note: The address must be fixed at call time, not re-evaluated at return time.

steps.title: Analyze the proposed implementation
steps.apply: B[i] = i initially, so A[B[i]] = A[i]. If i is unchanged throughout P, this writes back to the correct location. However if i is modified inside P (e.g. i becomes 5), then at return time i=5, and A[B[i]] = A[B[5]] = A[5], which is different from the original A[2].
steps.note: The implementation fails to lock in the original index at call time.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Because i might change inside P, the copy-back goes to the wrong element. The implementation is incorrect in general.
steps.note: A correct implementation would save the index j = i at call time and always copy back to A[j].

solution.result: The implementation is incorrect because if i changes inside P, the copy-back target A[B[i]] = A[i] refers to the new (post-call) value of i rather than the original index, producing an incorrect result.

questions.formula_note: Call-by-value-result (copy-in/copy-out): the l-value (address) of the actual argument must be captured at call time; using it again at return can give wrong results if the index expression changes during execution.

questions.question:
In which of the following case(s) is it possible to obtain different results for call-by-reference and call-by-name parameter passing?

A. Passing an expression as a parameter
B. Passing an array as a parameter
C. Passing a pointer as a parameter
D. Passing an array element as a parameter
questions.to_find: The cases where call-by-reference and call-by-name can produce different results

understand.plain: Call-by-reference passes the address of the actual argument; inside the procedure, the parameter is an alias for that variable. Call-by-name is a lazy/textual substitution mechanism where the actual argument expression is re-evaluated every time the formal parameter is used. These two differ when the actual argument is an expression (not a simple variable) or when the expression's value can change between uses (e.g., an array element where the index changes).

keywords.explain: The address of the actual argument is passed. The formal parameter is an alias for the actual variable. Works correctly only for l-values (addressable locations). Expressions do not have addresses.
keywords.example: P(a+b): under call-by-reference this is illegal/undefined since a+b has no address.

keywords.explain: The actual argument expression is substituted textually (like a macro) and re-evaluated every time the formal parameter is used inside the procedure.
keywords.example: P(A[i]) with call-by-name: every use of k inside P re-evaluates A[i] with the current value of i.

keywords.explain: Call-by-reference evaluates the address once at call time; call-by-name re-evaluates the expression each use. These differ when the expression involves variables that change.
keywords.example: If i changes inside P, A[i] under call-by-name gives A[new_i], but call-by-reference always gives the element at the original index.

understand.visual_alt: Four cases: (A) expression a+b — cannot be passed by reference (no address), differs from call-by-name; (B) array name — both give same base address; (C) pointer — both pass address, same; (D) array element A[i] — if i changes, call-by-name re-indexes, call-by-reference does not.

given.aim: Identify which parameter forms can yield different results under call-by-reference vs call-by-name.

terms.meaning: An arithmetic expression like a+b has no memory address, so call-by-reference is undefined/illegal. Call-by-name evaluates the expression each time.
terms.example: P(x+1): call-by-name re-evaluates x+1 at each use of k; call-by-reference has no l-value for x+1 — semantics differ.
terms.connects: This is a definite case where the two mechanisms differ.

terms.meaning: A[i] has an address (the address of that element), so call-by-reference works. But if i changes inside P, call-by-name re-evaluates A[i] with new i, while call-by-reference still accesses the original A[old_i].
terms.example: i=2 at call, P modifies i to 5. Call-by-reference: k always accesses A[2]. Call-by-name: k accesses A[5] after i changes.
terms.connects: This is a definite case where the two mechanisms differ.

terms.meaning: Passing an entire array or a pointer — both call-by-reference and call-by-name give the same base address since neither changes what is being pointed at.
terms.example: P(A) or P(p): the name A or p is a simple variable; re-evaluating it gives the same value each time.
terms.connects: B and C do not produce differences between the two mechanisms.

given.plan: For each option, determine whether call-by-reference and call-by-name give the same or different behavior. The key distinguishing factor is whether the actual argument is an expression (no l-value) or contains a subexpression that can change between uses.

steps.title: Analyze option A — expression as parameter
steps.apply: An expression like a+b is not an l-value; call-by-reference cannot be applied (it requires an address). Call-by-name substitutes the expression textually and re-evaluates it each use. These behave differently: call-by-name computes the expression's current value each time k is read, while call-by-reference would not be valid. Result: DIFFER.
steps.note: In languages that allow passing expressions by reference (as rvalue references), the behaviors still differ because call-by-name re-evaluates and call-by-reference captures a snapshot.

steps.title: Analyze option B — entire array as parameter
steps.apply: Passing an array name passes the base address. Whether by reference or by name, evaluating the array name gives the same address every time (the name doesn't change). Result: SAME.
steps.note: The array contents can be modified through either mechanism, but the mechanism itself produces the same access.

steps.title: Analyze option C — pointer as parameter
steps.apply: A pointer variable p is a simple variable with an address. Call-by-reference passes &p; call-by-name re-evaluates p each use. If p itself doesn't change inside P, both give the same value. Even if p changes inside P, the same aliasing effects apply to both. In standard scenarios the result is the SAME.
steps.note: Pointers can be tricky, but the mechanism difference itself (ref vs name) on a simple variable does not cause divergence.

steps.title: Analyze option D — array element as parameter
steps.apply: P(A[i]): call-by-reference evaluates the address of A[i] once at call time (say i=2, so address of A[2]). Call-by-name substitutes A[i] textually, so every use of k re-evaluates A[i] with the current value of i. If i changes inside P (e.g., a loop or assignment), call-by-name accesses a different element than call-by-reference. Result: DIFFER.
steps.note: Classic example: Jensen's device in ALGOL exploits call-by-name with array element parameters.

steps.title: Collect answers
steps.apply: Options where results can differ: A (expression) and D (array element). Options B and C do not differ.
steps.note: Both A and D are correct choices for this MSQ.

solution.result: Call-by-reference and call-by-name can produce different results when passing an expression (A) or an array element (D) as a parameter.

questions.formula_note: Call-by-name (textual/macro substitution) vs call-by-reference (address passing): they differ when the actual argument is a non-l-value expression or an indexed expression whose index changes inside the procedure (Jensen's device).
